summary: Gruber marks the iPhone's tenth anniversary by reflecting on its January 2007 introduction and its unprecedented impact on the world. He revisits Steve Jobs's iconic 'three revolutionary products' keynote framing, arguing that even Jobs underestimated what the iPhone would become. Gruber contends the iPhone transcended being merely industry-changing to become genuinely world-changing — the inflection point where personal computing truly became personal. He catalogs the industries and product categories the iPhone has displaced or created, from point-and-shoot cameras to ride-sharing services. He concludes that no product in the computing age compares in societal or financial impact, and we may never see anything like it again.
key_points: The iPhone introduction keynote remains uniquely compelling even on repeated viewings because Jobs's conviction matched the magnitude of what he was unveiling
Even Jobs underestimated the iPhone — it wasn't merely industry-changing but world-changing, the true inflection point for personal computing
The original iPhone felt impossible: shrinking a Unix workstation OS to a phone, achieving fluid touch scrolling, and wresting carrier control all seemed unbelievable simultaneously

The iPhone's impact was unfathomably deep — it killed or disrupted point-and-shoot cameras, GPS devices, portable music players, Nokia, BlackBerry, and enabled entirely new categories like ride-sharing
Ten years in, the iPhone's full potential still hasn't been tapped, and no product in the computing age compares in societal or financial impact
money_quotes: The iPhone was the product Apple had been founded to create — the epitome of everything both of Apple's founding Steves stood for and obsessed about.
With hindsight, though, I think even Jobs himself underestimated what the iPhone was.
The iPhone changed the world. There is no way to overstate it.
The iPhone was the inflection point where 'personal computing' truly became personal.
The iPhone was the only product introduction I've ever experienced that felt impossible.
The makers of ever-more-computer-like gadgets were simply unable to compete with ever-more-gadget-like computers.
No product in the computing age compares to the iPhone in terms of societal or financial impact. Few products in the history of the world compare. We may never see anything like it again.
key_insight: The iPhone wasn't just another industry-disrupting Apple product — it was the singular inflection point where personal computing truly became personal, and ten years later its full impact still hasn't been fully realized.
